required.title: Godel, Escher, Bach: An Eternal Golden Braid
required.author: Douglas Hofstadter

suggested.title: Introduction to the Theory of Computation
suggested.author: Michael Sipser

supplementary.title: Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs
supplementary.author: Abelson and Sussman

competence_markers.description: Can model a social, economic, or biological system computationally — identifying inputs, processes, outputs, and feedback loops
Can explain the halting problem in their own words and articulate its implications beyond computer science
Can narrate Godel's first incompleteness theorem and identify "blind spots" in real formal systems
Can explain how a specific AI/ML system works at a technical level, including its capabilities and failure modes
Can analyze incentive structures as functions, identifying divergence between intended and actual outputs
Can identify network effects, dependency structures, and critical paths in real-world systems
Can critically evaluate a computational model — articulating what it captures, what it misses, and where it misleads
Can apply computational thinking fluently to any domain, seeing structure, process, and algorithm in diverse systems
Demonstrates the habit of asking "what is the algorithm behind this outcome?" when encountering complex phenomena
Can articulate the transferable advantage of computational thinking — why it is a mode of perception rather than a technical skill

narration_prompts.prompt: Choose a social or political system and explain its incentive structure as a function. What are the inputs and outputs? Where do the intended outputs diverge from the actual ones?
Explain the halting problem in your own words. Why can't it be solved? What does this mean for the limits of formal analysis in general?
Describe a biological process as a computation. What makes the biological "algorithm" similar to a digital one? What makes it fundamentally different?
Explain Godel's first incompleteness theorem and give an example of a "blind spot" in a formal system you have encountered — a true statement the system cannot capture or prove.
Explain how a specific AI system works at a technical level. What is it actually doing? Where does it succeed, where does it fail, and why?
You have modeled a complex system computationally. What did the model reveal that you could not see without it? What did the model miss that turned out to be important?
Make the case that computational thinking is a *mode of perception* rather than a technical skill. What do you see now that you did not see before you developed this capacity?

cross_connections.description: — Mathematical maturity is essential for engaging with computability, complexity theory, and Godel's theorems. Growth rates, combinatorics, and proof structure are shared territory.

— Scientific modeling is computational modeling. Understanding the relationship between a model and reality — what the model captures and what it loses — is the central epistemological concern of both domains.
— Godel's theorems are theorems of formal logic. Understanding them transforms how you think about any formal system of rules, including ideological frameworks.
— Markets as distributed algorithms, supply chains as dependency graphs, business processes as state machines — computational thinking applied to economic systems is a core advantage.
— Complex fabrication and engineering projects require decomposition, dependency analysis, and systematic debugging at scale.
— Modeling arguments as logical structures, seeing rhetorical strategies as algorithms of persuasion, understanding how information propagates through networks.
— The computational theory in this packet provides the intellectual foundation for understanding every digital system.
— Civilizations as complex systems subject to computational analysis; historical processes as algorithms operating on populations over time.

content.why_this_matters:
You have learned to see sequences, patterns, and procedures. You have mastered formal tools — pseudocode, flowcharts, state machines, decomposition. You have developed intuition for computational complexity. Now comes the most powerful move: applying computational thinking as a lens through which to understand entire systems — social, economic, biological, mathematical — and confronting the profound limits of computation itself.

This is where Dijkstra's insight reaches its full expression. Computational thinking is not a technical skill for programmers. It is a mode of perception. The person who can model a market as a distributed algorithm, see a bureaucracy as an analyzable process, recognize DNA as an encoding system, and understand that evolution operates as a search algorithm — that person sees structure where others see chaos, process where others see events, algorithm behind outcome. This is the transferable advantage that no technological disruption can make obsolete, because it is the thinking *behind* the technology.

But this packet does not stop at the power of computation. It also confronts its limits — and those limits are not engineering constraints that will be solved with faster processors. They are mathematical certainties. Turing proved that there are problems no algorithm can solve. Godel proved that any sufficiently powerful formal system contains true statements it cannot prove. These are not abstract curiosities. They are fundamental facts about the nature of knowledge, certainty, and systems of rules — facts that reshape how a serious thinker approaches every domain.
content.what_this_develops:
1. The ability to model social, economic, and biological systems computationally — seeing incentive structures as functions, networks as graphs, processes as algorithms
2. Understanding of AI and machine learning from the inside — not as black-box magic but as pattern recognition, function approximation, and optimization
3. Genuine comprehension of the limits of computation — the halting problem, uncomputability, and their implications beyond computer science
4. Engagement with Godel's incompleteness theorems and their meaning for any system of formal rules
5. The capacity to apply algorithmic reasoning to any domain — seeing structure, process, and algorithm in every system
6. Critical evaluation of computational models — understanding what models capture and what they necessarily leave out
7. The fully realized transferable advantage: computational thinking as a permanent mode of perception
content.core_content:
### Modeling Social Systems Computationally

Every social system can be analyzed through a computational lens. This does not mean reducing human behavior to code — it means using the tools of computational thinking to reveal structure that is otherwise invisible.

**Incentive structures as functions.** A function takes an input and produces an output. An incentive structure does the same: given a set of rules and rewards, people produce predictable behavior. Tax policy is a function: input the rules, output the economic behavior. School grading is a function: input the grading criteria, output the student behavior (which may or may not be actual learning — the function maps to the *measured* output, not necessarily the desired one). Analyzing incentive structures as functions lets you ask the crucial question: *What behavior does this structure actually incentivize, regardless of what it claims to incentivize?*

**Network effects as exponential growth.** Social networks, communication systems, and marketplaces exhibit network effects — each additional participant increases the value for all existing participants. A phone is useless if you are the only person who has one. With two people, there is one possible connection. With five people, ten connections. With a hundred people, nearly five thousand. This is not linear growth — it follows a combinatorial pattern. Understanding network effects computationally explains why platforms tend toward monopoly, why languages resist replacement, and why social movements have tipping points.

**Bureaucratic processes as analyzable algorithms.** Every bureaucratic process — applying for a permit, navigating a legal system, enrolling in an institution — is an algorithm. It has inputs (your documents, your qualifications), processes (review steps, approval gates), decision points (accepted/rejected), and outputs. Modeling these as flowcharts or state machines reveals their actual structure: where bottlenecks exist, where decisions are made, where the process is efficient and where it is wasteful. The person who can see a bureaucracy as an algorithm can navigate it, critique it, and redesign it.

### Modeling Economic Systems as Computational Processes

Economics is rich with computational structure once you learn to see it.

**Market prices as distributed algorithms.** A market price is not set by any single person or entity. It emerges from millions of individual decisions — each buyer and seller acting on local information. This is a *distributed algorithm* — a computation performed by many independent agents with no central coordinator, yet producing a coherent output (the price). Understanding markets this way clarifies both their power (they aggregate vast amounts of information efficiently) and their failure modes (they can only aggregate the information participants actually have; they reflect biases, asymmetries, and irrationalities in the inputs).

**Supply chains as dependency graphs.** A supply chain is a dependency graph: raw materials must be extracted before they can be processed, processed before they can be assembled, assembled before they can be shipped. The critical path through this graph determines the minimum time from raw material to finished product. Disruptions propagate along dependency chains — a delay in one node cascades to everything downstream. This is the same dependency analysis you learned in Weathering Storms decomposition, applied at industrial scale.

**Arbitrage as pattern recognition.** Arbitrage — buying where something is cheap and selling where it is expensive — is pattern recognition applied to price data. The arbitrageur is an algorithm: scan for price discrepancies, evaluate transaction costs, execute trades where the margin is positive. Understanding arbitrage computationally demystifies financial markets and reveals that much of finance is, at its core, search and optimization.

### Modeling Biological Processes Computationally

Biology is perhaps the most stunning domain for computational thinking, because life itself operates through processes that are genuinely computational.

**DNA as encoding.** DNA is an information encoding system. Four bases (A, T, G, C) encode instructions for building proteins, just as binary digits (0, 1) encode instructions for a computer. The genetic code has error correction (redundancy in the codon table), compression (overlapping reading frames), and version control (epigenetic marks that modify expression without changing the underlying code). This is not metaphor — it is literal description. The cell is reading and executing encoded instructions.

**Protein folding as computation.** A protein is a chain of amino acids that folds into a specific three-dimensional shape, and the shape determines the function. Predicting the shape from the sequence is a computational problem of extraordinary difficulty — the number of possible configurations is astronomically large. The cell "solves" this problem through physical chemistry in milliseconds. Understanding this as a computational process illuminates both the power of biological computation and the limits of our ability to simulate it.

**Evolution as a search algorithm.** Evolution searches the space of possible organisms for those that survive and reproduce. It uses variation (mutation and recombination) to explore new possibilities and selection (survival and reproduction) to retain what works. This is structurally identical to a class of computational search algorithms called *evolutionary algorithms*. Understanding evolution this way clarifies its power (it explores vast possibility spaces), its limitations (it has no foresight; it optimizes for current conditions, not future ones), and its timescale (searching vast spaces takes vast time).

**The immune system as pattern matching.** Your immune system maintains a library of molecular patterns (antigens) associated with threats. When it encounters a new molecule, it checks for matches. If a match is found, it triggers a response. If a novel threat appears, the adaptive immune system generates variants, tests them, and retains effective ones — a process strikingly similar to machine learning. Your body is running a pattern-matching algorithm continuously.

### The Limits of Computation

Here the intellectual terrain shifts from "look at all the things computational thinking can do" to "here is what it provably cannot do." These limits are not temporary engineering constraints. They are permanent, mathematical facts about the nature of computation.

**The halting problem.** In 1936, Alan Turing proved that no general algorithm can determine, for every possible program and input, whether that program will eventually halt (finish) or run forever. This is not a claim that we are not smart enough — it is a proof that such an algorithm is logically impossible. The proof is elegant: assume such an algorithm exists, construct a program that uses it to contradict itself, conclude that the assumption was false.

The implications extend far beyond computer science. The halting problem means there are well-defined questions that no systematic procedure can answer. It places a hard boundary on what formal analysis can achieve. Any system complex enough to be interesting is complex enough to contain behaviors that cannot be predicted by any general method.

**Uncomputability.** The halting problem is just one example of an *uncomputable* problem. There are infinitely many others. For any sufficiently expressive formal system, there are questions that are well-defined but unanswerable by any algorithm. This is not a failure of technology — it is a feature of mathematics.

**Godel's incompleteness theorems.** In 1931, Kurt Godel proved two theorems that shook the foundations of mathematics:

*First theorem:* Any consistent formal system powerful enough to express basic arithmetic contains true statements that cannot be proved within the system. No matter how many axioms you add, there will always be more truths the system cannot reach.

*Second theorem:* No consistent formal system powerful enough to express basic arithmetic can prove its own consistency. You cannot use the rules to prove that the rules are reliable.

These theorems mean that every sufficiently powerful system of rules has blind spots — truths it cannot capture, and a consistency it cannot verify. This applies to mathematical systems, logical systems, legal codes, and any formal framework complex enough to be useful. The implications for certainty, completeness, and the limits of formal reasoning are profound.

### Connection to Artificial Intelligence

Understanding AI from the computational thinking perspective means seeing it not as magic or existential threat but as a set of specific techniques with specific capabilities and specific limits.

**Machine learning as pattern recognition at scale.** A machine learning system takes large amounts of data, identifies patterns in that data, and uses those patterns to make predictions about new data. It is, at its core, what you have been doing since Stretching Branches — pattern recognition — performed at a scale and speed no human can match. But the patterns it finds are statistical correlations. It does not understand *why* the patterns exist. It does not reason about causation. It excels within its training distribution and can fail spectacularly outside it.

**Neural networks as function approximation.** A neural network learns to approximate a function — given input X, produce output Y — by adjusting millions of internal parameters based on examples. It is a universal function approximator: given enough parameters and enough data, it can approximate any function to any desired accuracy. This is powerful but also misleading, because approximating a function is not the same as understanding the system that generates the function. A neural network that predicts weather does not understand atmospheric physics. It has found patterns in data.

**The critical question.** The computationally literate person asks: What training data was used? What patterns did it find? What is the error rate? Where does it fail? What does it optimize for, and is that the same as what I care about? What does it *not know* that is relevant? These questions are only possible when you understand AI from the inside rather than treating it as a black box.

### The Fully Realized Transferable Advantage

You have now arrived at the destination this entire sub-domain has been building toward. Computational thinking, fully developed, is not a subject — it is a way of seeing. You see:

- **Structure** where others see chaos — the algorithm behind the outcome, the pattern beneath the noise, the state machine inside the institution
- **Process** where others see events — not "the market crashed" but "the distributed algorithm that computes prices received inputs that caused this output"
- **Limits** where others see infinite possibility — not "we just need a better algorithm" but "this class of problems is provably intractable"
- **Models** where others see reality — and you understand that every model is a simplification, useful for some purposes and misleading for others

This mode of perception is yours permanently. No technological change can obsolete it because it is the thinking that creates and evaluates technology. No career disruption can take it from you because it applies to every domain. It is, in the deepest sense, sovereign knowledge — knowledge that depends on nothing outside your own mind.
content.formative_practices:
1. **The Incentive Structure Analysis:** Choose a real institution or system you participate in (a school, a sport league, a community organization, a government program). Model its incentive structure as a function: what are the inputs (rules, rewards, penalties), and what behavior do they actually produce? Compare the *intended* output with the *actual* output. Where do they diverge? Why? Narrate your analysis in depth.

2. **The Biological Computation Study:** Choose one biological process — DNA replication, immune response, evolution, protein folding, neural signaling — and study it as a computational process. What is the input? What is the algorithm? What is the output? Where does the biological "computation" exceed what our digital computers can match? Where does it fall short? Present your analysis with diagrams.

3. **The Halting Problem Engagement:** Study Turing's proof that the halting problem is unsolvable. Narrate the proof in your own words. Then engage with the implications: What does it mean for prediction? For certainty? For any system that tries to analyze itself? Connect this to at least one non-computational domain where the same kind of limit appears.

4. **The Godel Study:** Read an accessible treatment of Godel's incompleteness theorems (Hofstadter's *Godel, Escher, Bach* Chapters I-VI is ideal). Narrate the first incompleteness theorem in your own words. Then discuss: What are the "Godel sentences" in systems you know — the true statements that the system cannot prove? Consider legal systems, moral frameworks, and scientific theories.

5. **The AI Demystification Project:** Choose a specific AI application (image recognition, language generation, recommendation systems, autonomous driving). Research how it actually works at a technical level — not the marketing description but the computational mechanism. What patterns does it find? What data was it trained on? Where does it fail? Present your findings to your mentor, demonstrating that you understand the system from the inside.

6. **The Systems Modeling Project:** Choose a complex system — a local economy, a political process, an ecosystem, a social media platform — and model it computationally. Create a formal representation using state machines, dependency graphs, or flowcharts. Identify feedback loops, critical paths, and failure modes. Where does the model illuminate? Where does it oversimplify? What does the model leave out that matters?

7. **The Transferable Advantage Essay:** Write a substantial essay (1,500+ words) arguing either for or against the following claim: "Computational thinking is the single most transferable intellectual skill." Draw on your experience across all tiers and across multiple domains. Engage honestly with counterarguments.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### Computational Modeling of Real Systems

**Developing well:** The learner independently identifies computational structures in real systems — incentive functions, network effects, dependency graphs, state machines — and builds formal models that reveal non-obvious features. They can articulate both what the model captures and what it misses. Their models are genuinely useful: they predict behavior, identify bottlenecks, or reveal failure modes.

**Developing slowly:** The learner can apply computational vocabulary to systems (calling something "an algorithm" or "a function") but the analysis remains superficial — labeling rather than modeling. Their models do not generate new insight; they restate what was already obvious.

**Diagnostic questions:** "Your model shows X. What does it predict that you did not already know?" "What feature of this system does your model fail to capture? Does that matter?" "If someone used your model to make a decision, where might they go wrong?"

### Engagement with Limits of Computation

**Developing well:** The learner can narrate Turing's halting problem proof and Godel's first incompleteness theorem in their own words with genuine understanding — not recitation but comprehension of *why* the results hold. They can identify analogous limits in non-computational domains. They hold the tension between "computation is immensely powerful" and "computation has hard limits" without collapsing into either naive optimism or nihilism.

**Developing slowly:** The learner can state the results ("some problems are unsolvable," "some truths are unprovable") but cannot explain why or engage with the proofs. They may overgeneralize ("nothing is certain") or dismiss the results as irrelevant abstractions.

**Diagnostic questions:** "Walk me through the halting problem proof. Where does the contradiction arise?" "Godel showed that any sufficiently powerful system has blind spots. What is a blind spot in [a system the learner knows well]?" "Does the halting problem mean we should give up trying to predict whether programs halt? Why or why not?"

### AI Comprehension from the Inside

**Developing well:** The learner can explain how specific AI/ML systems actually work — what data they train on, what patterns they learn, how they make predictions, where they fail. They understand the difference between correlation and causation in ML systems. They can articulate why a system that performs well on training data may fail in deployment. They are neither credulous about AI capabilities nor dismissively fearful.

**Developing slowly:** The learner has opinions about AI (it will save or destroy us) but cannot explain how any specific system actually works. Their understanding is shaped by marketing and journalism rather than by technical comprehension.

**Diagnostic questions:** "How does [specific AI system] actually make its predictions?" "What training data was used, and what biases might that data contain?" "Give me an example of when this system would fail, and explain why."

### Cross-Domain Transfer

**Developing well:** The learner spontaneously applies computational thinking to new domains without being prompted. They see dependency structures in project planning, incentive functions in social systems, search algorithms in research processes. The transfer is genuine — not forced analogy but real insight generated by the computational lens.

**Developing slowly:** The learner can apply computational thinking when explicitly asked ("model this system as...") but does not reach for it independently. The tools remain compartmentalized rather than integrated into their general thinking.

**Diagnostic questions:** "When you encountered [a recent challenge in another domain], did computational thinking tools help you? How?" "Where in your life this week have you noticed an algorithm at work?" "What system do you interact with regularly that you have never modeled computationally? Try it now."
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Computational Reductionism

The most dangerous pitfall at Standing Ground is the belief that because everything *can* be modeled computationally, the computational model *captures* everything that matters. Human relationships are not fully described by network graphs. Economic behavior is not fully described by optimization functions. Consciousness is not explained by saying "the brain is a computer." Computational models are powerful tools for seeing structure, but they are maps, not territories. Press the learner whenever their model claims more explanatory power than it possesses.

### Overgeneralizing Godel and Turing

"Godel proved that nothing is certain" and "Turing proved that no problem can be solved" are both wrong. Godel proved something very specific: that any consistent formal system powerful enough to express basic arithmetic contains true statements it cannot prove. Turing proved something very specific: that no general algorithm can decide the halting problem for all programs. These are precise results with precise scope. They are profound, but they are not licenses for epistemic despair. Correct overgeneralizations firmly and direct the learner back to the actual proofs.

### Settling for Summaries

The difference between a learner who has read Hofstadter's treatment of Godel and one who has read a Wikipedia summary is immediately apparent in narration. The former can explain *why* the result holds, engage with the proof strategy, and extend the reasoning to new domains. The latter can state the conclusion and nothing more. Insist on primary sources and deep engagement. If the learner's narration is thin, the engagement is thin — assign the reading again with specific questions to answer.

### Treating AI as Either Magic or Threat

The culturally dominant framings of AI — utopian savior or existential threat — are both computationally illiterate. The learner at Standing Ground should understand AI as a specific set of techniques with specific strengths and specific weaknesses. When the learner makes sweeping claims about AI ("it will replace all jobs," "it cannot really think"), redirect toward specifics: "Which techniques? What data? What limitations? What assumptions?"

### Neglecting the Worldview Integration

The entire point of Standing Ground is that computational thinking becomes a *worldview* — a permanent mode of perception, not a subject to be studied and set aside. If the learner is doing excellent work within "computational thinking time" but not applying these tools in their other packets and projects, the integration has not happened. Actively look for opportunities to connect 4.2.8 work to everything else the learner is doing.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: "Explain a social system's incentive structure as a function."
**Strong:** "The college admissions system functions as: input a student's GPA, test scores, extracurriculars, and essays; output an accept/reject decision. But the function the system *actually* computes is different from the function it *claims* to compute. Because admissions optimizes for institutional ranking signals — acceptance rate, average test scores, yield rate — the actual function incentivizes students to optimize for metrics rather than genuine learning. The divergence between the stated function (identify promising scholars) and the actual function (optimize institutional metrics) explains most of the dysfunction in the system."
**Weak:** "The system has incentives that are like functions." (Press: which system? what inputs? what outputs? where do intended and actual outputs diverge?)

### Prompt 2: "Explain the halting problem."
**Strong:** "Turing asked: can we build an algorithm H that takes any program P and input I and determines whether P halts on I? He proved we cannot, by construction. Suppose H exists. Build a program D that takes a program P as input, runs H on (P, P), and does the opposite — if H says P halts on P, then D loops forever; if H says P loops, then D halts. Now run D on itself. If D(D) halts, then H said D loops on D, so D should loop — contradiction. If D(D) loops, then H said D halts on D, so D should halt — contradiction. Either way, H gave the wrong answer. So H cannot exist. The implication is that there are well-defined questions that no systematic procedure can answer — not because we are not clever enough, but because the questions are structured in a way that makes them unanswerable by any algorithm."
**Weak:** "Turing proved that some programs cannot be analyzed." (This is too vague. Press: how did he prove it? what is the contradiction? what exactly is uncomputable?)

### Prompt 3: "Describe a biological process as a computation."
**Strong:** "Evolution is a search algorithm operating on the space of possible genomes. Variation (mutation, recombination) is the exploration step — generating new candidate solutions. Selection (survival and reproduction) is the evaluation step — retaining candidates that score well on the fitness function defined by the current environment. Over generations, the population converges toward well-adapted organisms. The key computational insight is that evolution has no foresight — it optimizes for current conditions, not future ones. It is a greedy algorithm. It can get stuck in local optima. And it is astronomically slow because the search space is so vast. Understanding these computational properties predicts specific biological phenomena: vestigial organs (leftover from previous optima), convergent evolution (independent searches finding similar solutions), and extinction (failure to adapt when the fitness landscape shifts faster than the search can track)."
**Weak:** "DNA is like a computer program." (This is a surface analogy. Press: in what specific ways? where does the analogy hold and where does it break down? what predictions does the computational model make?)

### Prompt 4: "Discuss Godel's first incompleteness theorem and identify a blind spot in a real system."
**Strong:** "Godel proved that any consistent formal system powerful enough to encode arithmetic contains true statements that cannot be proved within that system. The proof works by encoding statements *about* the system within the system itself — including the statement 'this statement is not provable in this system.' If the system is consistent, this statement is true but unprovable. A real-world example: any legal code complex enough to be useful will contain situations that the code does not clearly resolve — not because the lawmakers were careless, but because the structure of sufficiently complex rule systems guarantees gaps. No amount of additional legislation can close all gaps because each new law creates new edge cases. This is not a failure of the legal system; it is a Godelian property of formal systems."
**Weak:** "Godel proved that math is incomplete." (This is too compressed. Press: what does 'incomplete' mean precisely? how did he prove it? what does it mean for other formal systems?)

### Prompt 5: "Explain how a specific AI system works at a technical level."
**Strong:** The learner can describe the actual mechanism — training data, loss function, architecture, optimization process — for a specific system. They can identify where and why the system fails. They can distinguish between what the system *does* (statistical pattern matching) and what it *does not do* (understand, reason causally, generalize beyond its training distribution).
**Weak:** The learner describes what the system does from a user perspective without any technical understanding of how it works.

### Prompt 6: "What did your computational model reveal and what did it miss?"
**Strong:** "My dependency graph of the local food system revealed that three suppliers are single points of failure — if any one closes, entire categories of food become unavailable at the farmers market. I would not have seen this without the model because the relationships are distributed across many vendors. But the model missed the social resilience of the community — when one vendor had supply problems last year, other vendors informally shared their supply, something no dependency graph would predict because it depends on relationships the graph does not capture."
**Weak:** "The model showed the structure of the system." (Press: what specific structure? what did you learn that you did not know before? what did it miss?)

### Prompt 7: "Computational thinking as a mode of perception."
**Strong:** "Before I developed computational thinking, I experienced bureaucracies as frustrating, markets as mysterious, and biological processes as miraculous. Now I see the algorithm inside each. A bureaucracy is a state machine with specific transitions I can map and navigate. A market is a distributed algorithm whose outputs I can analyze by understanding its inputs. Evolution is a search algorithm whose properties predict specific biological phenomena. This does not make them less remarkable — understanding evolution as computation makes it *more* remarkable, not less. But it means I can engage with these systems analytically rather than just experiencing them. The perception is permanent because it is not tied to any technology or tool — it is a way of seeing that works on anything with structure, which is everything."
**Weak:** "I see things computationally now." (Ask: what specifically? give me a concrete before-and-after. what do you see now that you did not see before?)
